# Transcrição: 02-Auction Optimisation- Page Engagement

- **Fonte:** 02-Auction Optimisation- Page Engagement
- **Duração:** 07:08
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:06]

Back in around early part of 2018, Facebook made a significant change in the algorithm to reward real engagement at the page level. So, you know, prior to 2017 going backwards, you could create virality on a page through engagement baiting. So that would be, you know, click here, like here, comment here type of thing to get some kind of reward. So a lot of people create engagement on their posts by saying, you know, comments, something below in their ads and trying to engage people by increasing their social proof. So hit the like button to enter this competition or things like that. So that was removed in around 2018. And at the same time, we noticed that on our ad accounts that had high engagement, a good number of fans, and a high number of posts frequency related to the page size, we were also seeing lower CPMs in our ad accounts. So if an ad account, if a page had, let's say 10,000 fans, and we were posting every day, and those posts were getting okay engagement. So, you know, if you think about 10,000 fans, and on average, you know, Facebook talks about or people talk about 1% reach on average for your fans. So from that 10,000, it's not a huge reach. But if your pages are getting anywhere between 25 and 100 organic likes and engagement comments, engaging comments on your posts, that's kind of okay. It's not, you know, could be better, but that's kind of like the baseline. Now I've seen many pages which have tens of thousands of fans, and their posts are getting engagements of one, two, three likes, very few comments, etc. There are many reasons why that could happen. Number one is that the type of fans being driven into that page and liking it, maybe it's been done artificially, which is a common tactic of those trying to inflate their page likes and doing it in the wrong way. Or it could be that the content of the posts are just not very good. Now if you want to use your page to get your CPMs down, it's important to understand that having people liking your page that are actually interested in your brand or your product or service is important, and then making sure the content is delivered to them, which engages them. It sounds obvious, but so many people miss this out. So page size dictates kind of how frequently your posts and kind of what your expectations are in terms of the number of likes and engagements on the post. Fan quality makes a huge difference. The post frequency as well is something that has an impact on your overall page quality. And also one of the other things is negative scores. So to give you an example, my personal brand page has been organically grown,

## [03:06 - 06:12]

so we haven't run a likes campaign, for example, or asked people to like it in terms of getting some kind of reward. But what we have done is when we've run ads and we've found people that are engaging in our posts, so for example, if we run an ad for one of our products or just an organic post, and that ad gets likes, we will invite those likers to like the page as well. So when you see a post and it's running as an ad, or organically as well, because sometimes posts get shared, you can click on the likes as long as you're logged in in the admin side of business manager, and invite those people to like your page. And that's how we have organically grown pages. And it's the it's a safer way to grow because if you're running ads and people like your ad anyway, then those are the fans that you want because they like your product or service. That over time sends a high quality signal back to Facebook that you have page fans that are engaged in your product or service. And therefore, when you're running ads, Facebook will favor you with lower CPMs. We've seen this time and time again, across our Facebook pages and our ad accounts when the Facebook page has a high quality of user engaged, when we have a good number of people as well. And we're pushing out good quality content, we see lower CPM. So if you are running ads right now, and you're not concentrating on your page content and frequency of content as well, chances are, even though if your performance is okay, chances are you're possibly paying more in terms of your CPM than you should be. So here's an example of a post that was put on my page where we wanted to test what I'm kind of looking for is kind of virality in terms of the engagement. So this post itself, you know, you can see it's had over 1000 likes, hearts, all that kind of stuff. About 283,000 people were reached. So from that, in terms of the number of likes, it's kind of okay, it's not not great. It's a few comments, and a good number of shares as well. But one of the key things that I look for when I'm running any kind of ad, and this also applies for kind of pushing page posts in terms of ads as well, is the negative feedback. You want to keep that as low as possible. Now, this post in terms of the reach likes, comments, etc. Two posts were hidden from the user. And one person said, I don't want to see anything from this person at all. That's a fairly low number. But if you are seeing that creep up, that's something you do need to keep an eye on as well. So one of the things I do is when I'm looking to land content on my Facebook pages is to do some testing in the background to see

## [06:12 - 07:08]

which posts are likely to engage. So if you imagine you've got a content strategy, or you might just randomly push content into your page, how do you know which content pieces are going to engage with your user? You could literally just post it. And if, you know, within an hour, it gets a good number of likes, keep it if not then delete it. Or you could run a small amount of ad testing in the background before you post some content on your page to know whether people are going to engage in it or not. That also has a second added benefit that if people are liking your content, you can also use that at the top of your funnel as well. So in the next part of page engagement, I'll show you a test that I like to run to test the engagement of a post either before I put it on my page or before I promote it.
